title: Build an Asteroids Game
courseTitle: Build Asteroids using Python and Pygame
readme:
# Build an Asteroids Game

We're going to build a simple video game, based on the classic [Asteroids](<https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Asteroids_(video_game)>). If you've never played before, you can take a look at this (slightly different from our) [version of the game](https://www.echalk.co.uk/amusements/Games/asteroidsClassic/ateroids.html).

![The finished project](https://storage.googleapis.com/qvault-webapp-dynamic-assets/course_assets/YmSwzVB-691x478.gif)

## [!windows] for Windows Users

> [!warning]
> If you try to complete this course without [WSL 2](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/wsl/install) installed on Windows, you're gonna have a bad time.

## Prerequisites

- Python 3.10+ installed (see the [Bookbot project](https://www.boot.dev/courses/build-bookbot) for help if you don't already have this)
- The [uv](https://docs.astral.sh/uv/getting-started/installation/) project/package manager
- Access to a Unix-like shell (e.g., `zsh` or `bash`)

## Learning Goals

The learning goals of this project are as follows:

1. To introduce you to multi-file Python projects
2. To get you used to referencing documentation (something you'll do often in the real world)
3. To show you a real-world use case for object-oriented programming
4. To have a ton of fun building a rewarding project!

The goal is _not_ to teach game development or the math required for physics simulations. As a result, there are some places where you'll simply copy-paste code that we wrote for you. Don't worry about trying to understand every detail if it's not interesting to you; you will be asked to write the parts that matter most.

## Assignment

To get started, make sure you have the [Boot.dev CLI](https://github.com/bootdotdev/bootdev) installed and working. Make sure you've installed `uv` (mentioned in Prerequisites) before running the tests.

**Run and submit** the CLI tests.

_The tests just ensure that the CLI is installed and configured correctly._

title: Installation
courseTitle: Build Asteroids using Python and Pygame
readme:
# Installation

We're going to be using [pygame](https://www.pygame.org/) and a [virtual environment](https://docs.python.org/3/library/venv.html) to develop our game.

## Pygame

Pygame is a module for developing games using Python. It provides simple functions and methods for us to draw images within a GUI window and handle user input.

> [!warning]
> Unfortunately, `pygame` doesn't yet support Python 3.14. Until it's updated, we recommend using Python 3.13 - make sure it's installed before continuing.

## Virtual Environment (venv)

Virtual environments are Python's way to keep dependencies (e.g., the `pygame` module) separate from other projects on our machine. For example, we need `pygame` version 2 for this project, but another project on your computer might require version 1.

As a best practice, each Python project on your machine should have its own virtual environment to keep them isolated from each other.

## Assignment

1. [ ] Create a new `uv` project on your computer.
   ```sh
   uv init --python 3.13 your-project-name
   cd your-project-name
   ```
   > [!info]
   > `--python 3.13` pins your project's Python version to `3.13`
2. [ ] Create a virtual environment at the top level of your project directory:
   ```sh
   uv venv
   ```
3. [ ] Activate the virtual environment:

   ```sh
   source .venv/bin/activate
   ```

   You should see `(your-project-name)` at the beginning of your terminal prompt – for example, mine is:

   ```text
   (Asteroids) wagslane@MacBook-Pro-2 Asteroids %
   ```

   > [!warning]
   > Make sure that your virtual environment is activated when running the game or using the bootdev CLI.

4. [ ] Add the `pygame` library as a project dependency:

   ```sh
   uv add pygame==2.6.1
   ```

   This tells Python that this project requires `pygame` with an exact version of `2.6.1`.

5. [ ] Make sure `pygame` is installed:
   ```sh
   uv run -m pygame
   ```

This will **result in an error** (the test expects an exit code of 1), but the output will show that `pygame` is installed.

**Submit** the CLI tests. There's no penalty on failure for this lesson.

> [!warning]
> If you're on WSL, you will probably need to install VcXsrv to run `pygame`. ([What is VcXsrv?](https://www.lenovo.com/us/en/glossary/vcxsrv/)) Install the [latest release](https://github.com/marchaesen/vcxsrv/releases). If you need help at any point, ask in the [community Discord](https://www.boot.dev/community).

title: Pygame
courseTitle: Build Asteroids using Python and Pygame
readme:
# Pygame

Now that we have `pygame` installed, let's write just a bit of [boilerplate](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Boilerplate_code) code so that we have a runnable program to build on.

## Assignment

1. [ ] Open `main.py`. It should've been created when you ran `uv init`.
2. [ ] On the first line, [import](https://docs.python.org/3/tutorial/modules.html) the `pygame` [module](https://docs.python.org/3/tutorial/modules.html). This will allow us to _use_ the `pygame` library in this file.
   ```py
   import pygame
   ```
3. [ ] Modify the main function from the default print statement to instead print:

   ```text
   Starting Asteroids with pygame version: VERSION
   ```

   Where `VERSION` is the version of `pygame` you have installed. Because we imported `pygame` at the top of the file, we can now dynamically access the [`pygame.version.ver`](https://www.pygame.org/docs/ref/pygame.html#pygame.version.ver) attribute.

   > [!warning]
   > Keep the `__name__ == "__main__"` codeblock at the bottom of the file. This line ensures that the `main` function is called only when this file is run directly; it won't run if it's imported as a module. It's considered the ["Pythonic"](https://docs.python.org/3/glossary.html#term-Pythonic) way to structure an executable program in Python.

4. [ ] Run the program with `uv run main.py`.

**Submit** the CLI tests using the [Boot.dev CLI](https://github.com/bootdotdev/bootdev). There's no penalty on failure for this lesson.

title: Modules
courseTitle: Build Asteroids using Python and Pygame
readme:
# Modules

It would be a _massive_ pain if we had to fit all of our code into a single file. So, let's organize our code into separate [modules](https://docs.python.org/3/tutorial/modules.html).

Since each `.py` file is a module, we can group related functions, variables, and classes together in a module. Then, other modules can import them using the syntax:

```py
from module_name import my_function
# or import everything: from module_name import *
```

## Constants

Games often have a lot of [magic numbers](<https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Magic_number_(programming)>) to represent things like player speeds, item costs, and attack damage. We'll use a dedicated module as a place to store those kinds of constant values.

## Assignment

1. [ ] Create a new file in your project called `constants.py` (in the same folder as `main.py`), and paste this in:
   ```py
   SCREEN_WIDTH = 1280
   SCREEN_HEIGHT = 720
   ```
2. [ ] Import `SCREEN_WIDTH` and `SCREEN_HEIGHT` from the `constants.py` file into `main.py`.
3. [ ] After the "Starting Asteroids..." message, also print out the `SCREEN_WIDTH` and `SCREEN_HEIGHT` values when `main.py` is run, using the format below:
   ```text
   Screen width: 1280
   Screen height: 720
   ```

**Run and submit** the CLI tests.

title: Logging Game State
courseTitle: Build Asteroids using Python and Pygame
readme:
# Logging Game State

We're about to use Pygame to open a [GUI](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Graphical_user_interface) window and draw things to it. That'll be fun, but how can we verify that the game logic is working correctly?

To make this easier for you we've provided you a **logging function**, which you just need to import and call in one place. It takes a snapshot of your game state about once per second, for up to 16 seconds or until the game exits. These snapshots will be saved to a file called `game_state.jsonl` (in the [JSON Lines](https://jsonlines.org/) format). Our `bootdev` CLI will use the logs to ensure your game is working correctly!

_Game developers use logging like this all the time for debugging and testing._

## Assignment

For now, just add the logging code to a file; we'll have you import and call it in the next lesson.

1. [ ] Create a new file named `logger.py` in the same directory as your `main.py` file. Paste in the code from the "Logging Code" block below, and save the file.
2. [ ] Add `game_state.jsonl` to your `.gitignore` file, to make sure logs don't get committed to your repository.
3. [ ] Install [`jq`](https://github.com/jqlang/jq) if you don't have it installed already. Some of our CLI commands will need it to format the JSON logs.

**Install `jq` on Debian-based (WSL) systems:**

```sh
sudo apt-get update
sudo apt-get install jq
```

**Install `jq` on macOS with Homebrew:**

```sh
brew install jq
```

**Run and submit** the CLI tests.

## Logging Code

```py
import inspect
import json
import math
from datetime import datetime

__all__ = ["log_state", "log_event"]

_FPS = 60
_MAX_SECONDS = 16
_SPRITE_SAMPLE_LIMIT = 10  # Maximum number of sprites to log per group

_frame_count = 0
_state_log_initialized = False
_event_log_initialized = False
_start_time = datetime.now()


def log_state():
    global _frame_count, _state_log_initialized

    # Stop logging after `_MAX_SECONDS` seconds
    if _frame_count > _FPS * _MAX_SECONDS:
        return

    # Take a snapshot approx. once per second
    _frame_count += 1
    if _frame_count % _FPS != 0:
        return

    now = datetime.now()

    frame = inspect.currentframe()
    if frame is None:
        return

    frame_back = frame.f_back
    if frame_back is None:
        return

    local_vars = frame_back.f_locals.copy()

    screen_size = []
    game_state = {}

    for key, value in local_vars.items():
        if "pygame" in str(type(value)) and hasattr(value, "get_size"):
            screen_size = value.get_size()

        if hasattr(value, "__class__") and "Group" in value.__class__.__name__:
            sprites_data = []

            for i, sprite in enumerate(value):
                if i >= _SPRITE_SAMPLE_LIMIT:
                    break

                sprite_info = {"type": sprite.__class__.__name__}

                if hasattr(sprite, "position"):
                    sprite_info["pos"] = [
                        round(sprite.position.x, 2),
                        round(sprite.position.y, 2),
                    ]

                if hasattr(sprite, "velocity"):
                    sprite_info["vel"] = [
                        round(sprite.velocity.x, 2),
                        round(sprite.velocity.y, 2),
                    ]

                if hasattr(sprite, "radius"):
                    sprite_info["rad"] = sprite.radius

                if hasattr(sprite, "rotation"):
                    sprite_info["rot"] = round(sprite.rotation, 2)

                sprites_data.append(sprite_info)

            game_state[key] = {"count": len(value), "sprites": sprites_data}

        if len(game_state) == 0 and hasattr(value, "position"):
            sprite_info = {"type": value.__class__.__name__}

            sprite_info["pos"] = [
                round(value.position.x, 2),
                round(value.position.y, 2),
            ]

            if hasattr(value, "velocity"):
                sprite_info["vel"] = [
                    round(value.velocity.x, 2),
                    round(value.velocity.y, 2),
                ]

            if hasattr(value, "radius"):
                sprite_info["rad"] = value.radius

            if hasattr(value, "rotation"):
                sprite_info["rot"] = round(value.rotation, 2)

            game_state[key] = sprite_info

    entry = {
        "timestamp": now.strftime("%H:%M:%S.%f")[:-3],
        "elapsed_s": math.floor((now - _start_time).total_seconds()),
        "frame": _frame_count,
        "screen_size": screen_size,
        **game_state,
    }

    # New log file on each run
    mode = "w" if not _state_log_initialized else "a"
    with open("game_state.jsonl", mode) as f:
        f.write(json.dumps(entry) + "\n")

    _state_log_initialized = True


def log_event(event_type, **details):
    global _event_log_initialized

    now = datetime.now()

    event = {
        "timestamp": now.strftime("%H:%M:%S.%f")[:-3],
        "elapsed_s": math.floor((now - _start_time).total_seconds()),
        "frame": _frame_count,
        "type": event_type,
        **details,
    }

    mode = "w" if not _event_log_initialized else "a"
    with open("game_events.jsonl", mode) as f:
        f.write(json.dumps(event) + "\n")

    _event_log_initialized = True
```

title: Game Loop
courseTitle: Build Asteroids using Python and Pygame
readme:
# Game Loop

Video games are generally built using a [game loop](https://gameprogrammingpatterns.com/game-loop.html). The simplest game loop has 3 steps:

1. Check for player inputs
2. Update the game world
3. Draw the game to the screen

To create a good user experience, these 3 steps need to happen _many times per second_.

## Assignment

1. [ ] In `main.py` import the `log_state` function from the `logger` module that you created in the last assignment:
   ```py
   from logger import log_state
   ```
2. [ ] Within the `main()` function, initialize pygame using the [`pygame.init()`](https://www.pygame.org/docs/ref/pygame.html#pygame.init) function at the beginning of your program.
3. [ ] Use pygame's [`display.set_mode`](https://www.pygame.org/docs/ref/display.html#pygame.display.set_mode) function to get a new instance of GUI window:
   ```py
   screen = pygame.display.set_mode((SCREEN_WIDTH, SCREEN_HEIGHT))
   ```
4. [ ] Create the game loop. For now, we'll only worry about step 3 from the list above: _drawing the game to the screen_.
   1. [ ] Use an [infinite while loop](https://realpython.com/python-while-loop/#infinite-loops) (e.g. `while True:`) for the game loop. At each iteration, it should:
      1. [ ] Call `log_state()` – no arguments needed.
      2. [ ] Add the following code, so that we can start processing the [pygame event queue](https://www.pygame.org/docs/ref/event.html). We won't handle any events yet, we'll just call `pass` so our program remains responsive:
         ```py
         for event in pygame.event.get():
             pass
         ```
      3. [ ] Use the screen's [`.fill`](https://www.pygame.org/docs/ref/surface.html#pygame.Surface.fill) method to fill the screen with a solid `"black"` color (you can literally just pass the string `"black"` to the method).
      4. [ ] Use pygame's [`display.flip()`](https://www.pygame.org/docs/ref/display.html#pygame.display.flip) method to refresh the screen. Be sure to call this last!
5. [ ] Run and test your game frequently as you write code to make sure it's working as expected. For now, you should see a black window open _and stay open_.
   ```sh
   uv run main.py
   ```
6. [ ] Close the game and kill the program with `Ctrl+C` in the terminal (not the close button in the window).
7. [ ] In your event queue loop, if the event type is a `pygame.QUIT` event, `return`. This will check if the user has closed the window, and exit the game loop if they do. _It will make the window's close button actually work_.
   ```py
   for event in pygame.event.get():
       if event.type == pygame.QUIT:
           return
   ```
8. [ ] Make a Git commit! It's a good idea to commit your progress whenever you get something new working.
9. [ ] After running the game program for a bit, then closing it, check that you have a non-empty `game_state.jsonl` file (but don't edit it manually). Most of the assignments for the remainder of this project will be tested against the log file.

**Run and submit** the CLI tests.

title: FPS
courseTitle: Build Asteroids using Python and Pygame
readme:
# FPS

As it stands, our game is re-drawing the screen as fast as it possibly can. This causes it to use a lot more CPU than it actually needs!

## Delta Time

The Greek letter delta (Δ) is often used to represent a change in a value in mathematics. In game development, we use "delta time" to represent the amount of time that has passed since the last frame was drawn. This value is useful to **decouple** the game's speed from the speed it's being drawn to the screen.

If your computer's CPU _speeds up_ (its speed is not constant, like a sprinter running as fast as they can), the asteroids shouldn't _also_ speed up. Conversely, if your computer _slows down_, the asteroids shouldn't also slow down: they may just move less smoothly.

## Assignment

FPS stands for [frames per second](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Frame_rate). We're going to restrict our game to draw a maximum of 60 times per second, or 60 FPS.

1. [ ] After initializing `pygame`, but before the game loop starts, create:
   1. [ ] A new clock object using [`pygame.time.Clock()`](https://www.pygame.org/docs/ref/time.html#pygame.time.Clock).
   2. [ ] A `dt` variable set to `0`.
2. [ ] At the end of each iteration of the game loop, call the [`.tick()`](https://www.pygame.org/docs/ref/time.html#pygame.time.Clock.tick) method on the clock object, and pass it `60`. It will pause the game loop until 1/60th of a second has passed.
3. [ ] The `.tick()` method also _returns_ the amount of time that has passed since the last time it was called: _the delta time_. Divide the return value by `1000` (to convert from milliseconds to seconds) and save it into the `dt` variable we created earlier.
4. [ ] Print the updated `dt` value to the console.
5. [ ] Re-run the game. You should still see the same black screen, but this time it should use less of your system's resources! You should _also_ see the `dt` value printed over and over to the console, it may even fluctuate a bit as your computer's CPU speed changes.
6. [ ] Remove the line that prints `dt` to the console (but leave the variable itself, we'll need it later) - it creates a lot of noise, we just wanted to see it working.

If everything is still working as you'd expect, let the game run for a few seconds so that multiple entries are written to `game_state.jsonl`, **run and submit** the CLI tests.

title: Sprites
courseTitle: Build Asteroids using Python and Pygame
readme:
# Sprites

In our game, asteroids are _visually_ represented as circles, and the player is a triangle. However, detecting collisions between circles and triangles is _hard_. To avoid this problem, we can cheat a bit: the player will secretly be a circle (for the purposes of [collision detection](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Games/Techniques/2D_collision_detection)).

![The player is secretly a circle](https://storage.googleapis.com/qvault-webapp-dynamic-assets/course_assets/vn3shY7-360x300.png)

_The red circle won't be visible in the game; we just need to know it exists._

> [!info]
> Throughout this project, we will provide some of the code for you, like the class below. We want you to focus on OOP concepts not the game physics. As such, we've pre-written some of the code you'll need, like the `CircleShape` class below.

## Assignment

In Pygame, there's a base [Sprite](https://www.pygame.org/docs/ref/sprite.html#pygame.sprite.Sprite) class that represents visual objects.

Let's create a `CircleShape` class that _inherits_ from `Sprite` to represent objects in our game that are treated as circles (even if they aren't, like the player's ship).

**Create a new `circleshape.py` file and paste in the following code:**

```py
import pygame

# Base class for game objects
class CircleShape(pygame.sprite.Sprite):
    def __init__(self, x, y, radius):
        # we will be using this later
        if hasattr(self, "containers"):
            super().__init__(self.containers)
        else:
            super().__init__()

        self.position = pygame.Vector2(x, y)
        self.velocity = pygame.Vector2(0, 0)
        self.radius = radius

    def draw(self, screen):
        # must override
        pass

    def update(self, dt):
        # must override
        pass
```

`CircleShape` extends the `Sprite` class to store 3 additional attributes specific to our game:

- position (x and y coordinates)
- [velocity](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Velocity)
- [radius](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radius).

Later we'll write subclasses of `CircleShape` and override the `draw` and `update` methods with the logic for each particular game object.

**Run and submit** the CLI tests from the **root of the project**.

title: Draw Player
courseTitle: Build Asteroids using Python and Pygame
readme:
# Draw Player

Let's draw a spaceship for the player!

> [!tip]
> When you use a constant or class from another file (like `constants.py`), remember to import it at the top of your file, for example: `from constants import PLAYER_RADIUS`.

## Assignment

1. [ ] Add the following values to `constants.py`:
   - `PLAYER_RADIUS = 20` (the radius of the player's ship)
   - `LINE_WIDTH = 2` (the width of the lines that draw the player's ship)
2. [ ] Create a new file called `player.py` with a `Player` class that inherits from `CircleShape`.
3. [ ] The `Player` constructor should take `x` and `y` integers as input, then:
   1. [ ] Call the parent class's constructor, passing in x, y, and the `PLAYER_RADIUS` (you'll need to `import` it from your constants)
   2. [ ] Create an attribute called `rotation`, initialized to `0`.
4. [ ] Paste the `triangle` method below into your `Player` class. A player will _look like_ a triangle, even though we'll use a circle to represent its [hitbox](https://simple.wikipedia.org/wiki/Collision_detection). The math of drawing a triangle can be a bit tricky, so we've written the method for you:
   ```py
   # in the Player class
   def triangle(self):
       forward = pygame.Vector2(0, 1).rotate(self.rotation)
       right = pygame.Vector2(0, 1).rotate(self.rotation + 90) * self.radius / 1.5
       a = self.position + forward * self.radius
       b = self.position - forward * self.radius - right
       c = self.position - forward * self.radius + right
       return [a, b, c]
   ```
5. [ ] To draw the player, override the `draw` method of `CircleShape`. It should take the `screen` object as a parameter, and call [`pygame.draw.polygon()`](https://www.pygame.org/docs/ref/draw.html#pygame.draw.polygon). It takes as inputs:
   - The `screen` object
   - A color (use `"white"`)
   - A list of points (use the list returned by a call to the `self.triangle()` function)
   - A line width (use the one in your `constants.py` file)
6. [ ] In your main function, instantiate a `Player` object. You can pass these values to the constructor to spawn it in the middle of the screen:
   - `x = SCREEN_WIDTH / 2`
   - `y = SCREEN_HEIGHT / 2`
7. [ ] Lastly, we need to re-render the player on the screen each frame, meaning inside our game loop. Use the `player.draw(screen)` method we just added to do so.

> [!tip]
> Draw the player _after_ filling the screen with black, but _before_ flipping the screen.

Run the game for **at least a few seconds**, so that the log file gets populated.

If everything worked, you should see a black screen with a white triangle in the middle. Progress! **Run and submit** the CLI tests.

title: Moving Around
courseTitle: Build Asteroids using Python and Pygame
readme:
# Moving Around

We want the spaceship to be able to move; but what does that _mean specifically_? Let's break it down:

1. If we press the move-left key, the ship should **rotate** to the left (i.e., counter-clockwise).
2. If we press the move-right key, the ship should **rotate** to the right (i.e., clockwise).
3. If we press the move-forward key, the ship should **move** forward.
4. If we press the move-backward key, the ship should **move** backward.

Left and right seem pretty similar. Let's try to write a small function to tackle those first!

## Assignment

1. [ ] Create a new constant in `constants.py` to represent the player's turn speed. I named mine `PLAYER_TURN_SPEED`, and give it a value of `300`.
2. [ ] Add a new `rotate` method to the `Player` class. It takes one argument: `dt` (I told you we'd use it!). When it's called, it should _add_ `PLAYER_TURN_SPEED * dt` to the player's current `rotation`.
3. [ ] Paste in the following `update` method to the `Player` class:

   ```py
       def update(self, dt):
           keys = pygame.key.get_pressed()

           if keys[pygame.K_a]:
               # ?
           if keys[pygame.K_d]:
               # ?
   ```

   > [!tip]
   > If you're using a non-[QWERTY](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/QWERTY) keyboard, you may want to change the keybindings in the code to something other than the W, A, S and D keys.

4. [ ] Update the missing lines to call the `rotate` method with the `dt` argument. To go left instead of right when `a` is pressed, you'll need to reverse `dt`... _how can you do that...?_
5. [ ] Hook the `update` method into the game loop by calling it on the player object each frame before rendering.
6. [ ] Run the game for at least a few seconds, and make sure the `a` and `d` keys rotate the player left and right, respectively.

If everything seems to be working, **run and submit** the CLI tests.

title: Moving
courseTitle: Build Asteroids using Python and Pygame
readme:
# Moving

Now we want the ship to move back and forth with the W and S keys.

## Assignment

1. [ ] Create a new constant in `constants.py` to represent the player speed called `PLAYER_SPEED`, and give it a value of `200`.
2. [ ] Add a new method to the `Player` class called `.move()`. If you get stuck, use the reference below. This method takes one argument: `dt`. It will modify the player's `position` using the following math:
   1. [ ] Start with a [unit vector](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Unit_vector) pointing straight down from `(0, 0)` to `(0, 1)`.
   2. [ ] _Rotate_ that vector by the player's rotation, so it's pointing in the same direction as the player.
   3. [ ] Multiply the vector by `PLAYER_SPEED * dt` so that the vector is the length the player should move during this frame.
   4. [ ] Add the vector to the player's `position` to move them.

   This is a course on programming, not vector math, so we've provided the math for you. All those words boil down to these lines of code:

   ```py
   unit_vector = pygame.Vector2(0, 1)
   rotated_vector = unit_vector.rotate(self.rotation)
   rotated_with_speed_vector = rotated_vector * PLAYER_SPEED * dt
   self.position += rotated_with_speed_vector
   ```

   > [!info]
   > `.rotate()` returns a _new_ vector – it doesn't modify the original. That's why we assign the result to `rotated_vector`.

   ![Vector Math](https://storage.googleapis.com/qvault-webapp-dynamic-assets/course_assets/o3MvzSe-1280x714.png)

3. [ ] Modify the `update` method in the `Player` class to call the `move` method when the W or S key is pressed. Remember to negate `dt` for the S key, so the player moves backward.
4. [ ] Run the game for at least a few seconds. You should now be able to fly around the screen with the W, A, S, and D keys!

If everything looks good, **run and submit** the CLI tests.

title: Groups
courseTitle: Build Asteroids using Python and Pygame
readme:
# Groups

We're manually calling `.update()` and `.draw()` on the `player` object at the moment, which means our game loop is going to get more and more cluttered as we add additional objects to the game. Fortunately, Pygame has a solution!

The [Group](https://www.pygame.org/docs/ref/sprite.html#pygame.sprite.Group) class is a container that holds and manages multiple game objects. We can organize our objects into various groups to track them more easily.

You can think of them as a sort of [Venn diagram](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Venn_diagram). An object can be in multiple groups at the same time! Eventually, our game's groups will look something like this:

![Venn-diagram of the groups we will eventually have](https://storage.googleapis.com/qvault-webapp-dynamic-assets/course_assets/sEC3wDu-758x448.png)

## Examples of Using Groups

Create a new empty group called `my_group`:

```py
my_group = pygame.sprite.Group()
```

Add all future instances of a `Player` class to two different groups (`group_a` and `group_b`):

```py
# Player is the name of the class, not an instance of it
# This must be done before any Player objects are created
Player.containers = (group_a, group_b)
```

You can iterate over objects in a group just like any other collection in Python:

```py
for thing in group:
    thing.do_something(some_value)
```

You may also call the `.update()` method for every member of a group by calling it on the group itself:

```py
group.update(dt)
```

## Assignment

1. [ ] Before the game loop starts, create two new empty groups in `main.py`:
   - [ ] `updatable` – this will hold all the objects that can be updated
   - [ ] `drawable` – this will hold all the objects that can be drawn
2. [ ] Add the `Player` class to the `updatable` and `drawable` groups _before_ the player object instance is created.
   ```py
   Player.containers = (updatable, drawable)
   ```
3. [ ] Update the game loop to use the new groups instead of the `Player` object directly.
   1. [ ] Call the `.update()` method on the "updatable" group.
   2. [ ] Loop over all "drawables" and `.draw()` them individually.
4. [ ] Run the game for at least a few seconds. It should look and behave the same as before, but now we're using groups to manage our objects.

If everything seems to be working, **run and submit** the CLI tests.

title: Asteroids
courseTitle: Build Asteroids using Python and Pygame
readme:
# Asteroids

A critical part of Asteroids is the... well, _asteroids_. Let's create another class to represent them.

Asteroids in our game are simply circles.

## Assignment

1. [ ] Create a new class called `Asteroid` in a new file called `asteroid.py` that inherits from `CircleShape`.
2. [ ] Give it a constructor with this signature, and inside it call the parent `CircleShape` constructor so the sprite is initialized correctly:
   ```py
   def __init__(self, x, y, radius):
       super().__init__(x, y, radius)
   ```
3. [ ] Override the `draw()` method to draw the asteroid using the [`pygame.draw.circle`](https://www.pygame.org/docs/ref/draw.html#pygame.draw.circle) function. It accepts:
   - The "surface" to draw on (the screen object)
   - The color of the circle ("white")
   - Its own position as the center
   - Its own radius
   - The width of the line to draw the circle (use `LINE_WIDTH` from `constants.py`)
4. [ ] Override the `update()` method so that it moves in a straight line at constant speed. On each frame, it should add `(self.velocity * dt)` to its position (get `self.velocity` from its parent class, `CircleShape`).
5. [ ] In `main.py` before the game loop starts, create a new empty [`pygame.sprite.Group`](https://www.pygame.org/docs/ref/sprite.html#pygame.sprite.Group) for the asteroids.
6. [ ] Like we did with the `Player` class, set the static `containers` field of the `Asteroid` class to the new `asteroids` group, as well as the `updatable` and `drawable` groups. This ensures that every instance of the `Asteroid` class is automatically added to these groups upon creation.
   ```py
   Asteroid.containers = (asteroids, updatable, drawable)
   ```
7. [ ] Add the following constants to `constants.py`:
   ```py
   ASTEROID_MIN_RADIUS = 20
   ASTEROID_KINDS = 3
   ASTEROID_SPAWN_RATE_SECONDS = 0.8
   ASTEROID_MAX_RADIUS = ASTEROID_MIN_RADIUS * ASTEROID_KINDS
   ```
8. [ ] I've written an `AsteroidField` class for you – it contains logic for spawning new asteroids. Create a new file called `asteroidfield.py` and paste in this code:

   ```py
   import pygame
   import random
   from asteroid import Asteroid
   from constants import *

   class AsteroidField(pygame.sprite.Sprite):
       edges = [
           [
               pygame.Vector2(1, 0),
               lambda y: pygame.Vector2(-ASTEROID_MAX_RADIUS, y * SCREEN_HEIGHT),
           ],
           [
               pygame.Vector2(-1, 0),
               lambda y: pygame.Vector2(
                   SCREEN_WIDTH + ASTEROID_MAX_RADIUS, y * SCREEN_HEIGHT
               ),
           ],
           [
               pygame.Vector2(0, 1),
               lambda x: pygame.Vector2(x * SCREEN_WIDTH, -ASTEROID_MAX_RADIUS),
           ],
           [
               pygame.Vector2(0, -1),
               lambda x: pygame.Vector2(
                   x * SCREEN_WIDTH, SCREEN_HEIGHT + ASTEROID_MAX_RADIUS
               ),
           ],
       ]

       def __init__(self):
           pygame.sprite.Sprite.__init__(self, self.containers)
           self.spawn_timer = 0.0

       def spawn(self, radius, position, velocity):
           asteroid = Asteroid(position.x, position.y, radius)
           asteroid.velocity = velocity

       def update(self, dt):
           self.spawn_timer += dt
           if self.spawn_timer > ASTEROID_SPAWN_RATE_SECONDS:
               self.spawn_timer = 0

               # spawn a new asteroid at a random edge
               edge = random.choice(self.edges)
               speed = random.randint(40, 100)
               velocity = edge[0] * speed
               velocity = velocity.rotate(random.randint(-30, 30))
               position = edge[1](random.uniform(0, 1))
               kind = random.randint(1, ASTEROID_KINDS)
               self.spawn(ASTEROID_MIN_RADIUS * kind, position, velocity)
   ```

9. [ ] In the `main.py` file, import the `AsteroidField` class and set its static `containers` field to _only_ the `updatable` group (it's not drawable, and it's not an asteroid itself).
10. [ ] After adding the container, create a new `AsteroidField` object.
11. [ ] Run the game for at least a few seconds, making sure that asteroids are spawning and moving across the screen, and that you didn't break your ship in the process.

If everything looks good, **run and submit** the CLI tests.

title: Collisions
courseTitle: Build Asteroids using Python and Pygame
readme:
# Collisions

You may have noticed that you can fly through asteroids without any consequences.

We need [_collision detection_](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Collision_detection). Now, this is a fairly deep topic, but we're going to keep things simple: we'll treat everything (including our triangular ship) as a circle when it comes to collisions.

Detecting a collision between two circles is simple:

1. Calculate the distance between the center of the two circles: "distance"
2. Calculate the radius of each circle: `r1` and `r2`.
3. If `distance` is less than or equal to `r1 + r2`, the circles are colliding. If not, they aren't!

![Demonstration of two circles colliding.](https://storage.googleapis.com/qvault-webapp-dynamic-assets/course_assets/fBvtpZU-959x481.png)

## Assignment

Everything that collides inherits from `CircleShape`, so that seems like a good place to write our collision logic. We also want to log when collisions happen, so we'll use one more function from that `logger.py` module.

1. [ ] Add a `collides_with(self, other)` method to `CircleShape` to check for collisions. It should take another circle shape as a parameter (aside from `self` of course) and return `True` or `False`.
   > [!tip]
   > Each `CircleShape` has a `position` property that is a [`pygame.Vector2`](https://www.pygame.org/docs/ref/math.html#pygame.math.Vector2). You can use the `position`'s [`distance_to`](https://www.pygame.org/docs/ref/math.html#pygame.math.Vector2.distance_to) method to get the distance between the two shapes, then use that distance to decide whether to return `True` or `False`.
2. [ ] Back in your main file, import the `log_event` function from the `logger` module.
3. [ ] Add `game_events.jsonl` to your `.gitignore` file.
4. [ ] **After the "update" step** in your game loop, iterate over all the objects in your `asteroids` group. Check if any of them collide with the player. If a collision is detected:
   1. [ ] Call `log_event("player_hit")`.
   2. [ ] Print `Game over!` to the console.
   3. [ ] End the game immediately with [`sys.exit()`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/sys.html#sys.exit). (Don't forget to import `sys`!)
5. [ ] Start the game, and run your ship into an asteroid! It should print `Game over!` to your console and exit.

> [!warning]
> It's okay for asteroids to simply pass through each other for now.

If that worked, **run and submit** the CLI tests.

title: Shooting
courseTitle: Build Asteroids using Python and Pygame
readme:
# Shooting

Let's make things more interesting by allowing the player to shoot at the asteroids.

!["Oh man. What are you doing with a gun in space?" - Armageddon (1998)](https://storage.googleapis.com/qvault-webapp-dynamic-assets/course_assets/vWIDwnV-675x327.png)

In our game, bullets:

- Are small circles
- Move at a constant speed in a straight line
- Split up asteroids when they collide with them
- Are spawned by player input (spacebar) and move in the direction the player is facing

## Assignment

1. [ ] Add a new `SHOT_RADIUS` constant and set it to `5`.
2. [ ] Create a new `Shot` class to represent a bullet in a new `shot.py` file.
   - It should _also_ inherit from `CircleShape`, similar to the `Asteroid` class.
   - Don't forget to override its `draw` and `update` methods, just like you did in the `Asteroid` class!
3. [ ] Set up a new `shots` group in your `main.py` initialization code.
4. [ ] Set the `containers` field of the `Shot` class to include your new `shots` group, along with the `drawable` and `updatable` containers, just like you did with your `Player` and `Asteroid` classes.
5. [ ] Add `PLAYER_SHOOT_SPEED` to your constants file, with a value of `500`.
6. [ ] In your `Player` class, add a new method called `shoot`. This method takes no parameters and does the following:
   1. [ ] Creates a new `Shot` at the current position of the player.
   2. [ ] Sets the shot's `.velocity` attribute:
      1. [ ] Start with a [`pygame.Vector2`](https://www.pygame.org/docs/ref/math.html#pygame.math.Vector2) of `(0, 1)`.
      2. [ ] [`.rotate()`](https://www.pygame.org/docs/ref/math.html#pygame.math.Vector2.rotate) the vector in the direction the player is facing.
      3. [ ] Scale it up (multiply by `PLAYER_SHOOT_SPEED`) to make it move faster.
7. [ ] In your `Player` class, handle the spacebar (`pygame.K_SPACE`) and call the `shoot` method when it's pressed.
8. [ ] Run your game for at least a few seconds, and fire some shots. Make sure the bullets are being created and moving in the correct direction.

> [!warning]
>
> - It's expected that holding the spacebar will create a constant stream of bullets. We'll fix that soon.
> - It's expected that bullets pass through asteroids without any consequences. We'll fix that soon, too.

If everything looks right, **run and submit** the CLI tests.

title: Rate Limit
courseTitle: Build Asteroids using Python and Pygame
readme:
# Rate Limit

You probably noticed that the weapon on the ship is currently as overpowered as the original [Zerg rush](<https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rush_(video_games)>). _Let's fix that._

## Assignment

1. [ ] Create a new variable in the `Player` constructor to act as a shot cooldown timer. It should start with a value of `0`.
2. [ ] Create a new `PLAYER_SHOOT_COOLDOWN_SECONDS` constant and set it to `0.3`.
3. [ ] When the player tries to shoot:
   1. [ ] If the current value of the timer is _greater than_ `0`, prevent the player from shooting.
   1. [ ] Otherwise, set the timer equal to `PLAYER_SHOOT_COOLDOWN_SECONDS`.
4. [ ] Every time `update` is called on the player, decrease the shoot timer by `dt` (the change in time).
5. [ ] Run your game for at least a few seconds, shooting to your heart's content. You should now be rate-limited to one shot every `0.3` seconds.

> [!warning]
> Bullets should still pass through asteroids without any consequences. _We'll fix that soon._

If things seem to be working, **run and submit** the CLI tests.

title: Destruction
courseTitle: Build Asteroids using Python and Pygame
readme:
# Destruction

Okay, so bullets are flying, but they don't do anything. Let's kill some asteroids!

There are three types of asteroids:

1. Large
2. Medium
3. Small

When a large asteroid is destroyed, it should split into two medium asteroids. When a medium asteroid is destroyed, it should split into two small asteroids. When a small asteroid is destroyed, it should disappear.

**For now, we'll just always make the asteroids disappear when they're destroyed.** We'll handle splitting later.

## Assignment

1. [ ] Add another collision check to the game loop. Loop over each asteroid, and for each asteroid, loop over each shot. If a shot and an asteroid collide:
   1. [ ] Call `log_event("asteroid_shot")`.
   2. [ ] Call the [`.kill()` method](https://www.pygame.org/docs/ref/sprite.html#pygame.sprite.Sprite.kill) on both objects (the shot and the asteroid) to remove them from the game.
      > [!info]
      > The `kill()` method is a built-in feature of Pygame sprites. It removes the "killed" object from all its groups so that the engine stops updating and drawing it.
2. [ ] Run the game for at least a few seconds, and make sure you can destroy asteroids by shooting them.

If everything looks right, **run and submit** the CLI tests.

title: Splitting
courseTitle: Build Asteroids using Python and Pygame
readme:
# Splitting

In the original game of Asteroids, part of the challenge comes from the fact that larger asteroids split into smaller, faster asteroids when you shoot them. Let's support that!

- Large asteroids split into two medium asteroids
- Medium asteroids split into two small asteroids
- Small asteroids disappear when destroyed

- After splitting, the new smaller asteroids move faster.
- After splitting, the new smaller asteroids move in **new random directions**. For example, in the diagram:

![Trajectory of new asteroids, after split.](https://storage.googleapis.com/qvault-webapp-dynamic-assets/course_assets/VOc5FFg-950x720.png)

A new angle for the new asteroids is chosen at random (between 20 and 50 degrees), then the new asteroids are created and start moving in those directions.

## Assignment

Instead of using `asteroid.kill()` in our game loop, we'll create and use a new `asteroid.split()` method.

**Add a new `.split()` method to the `Asteroid` class.** It should:

1. [ ] Immediately `.kill()` itself (think about it: _this_ asteroid is _always_ destroyed, but _maybe_ we'll spawn new smaller ones, depending on its size).
2. [ ] If the radius of the asteroid is less than or equal to `ASTEROID_MIN_RADIUS`, just `return`; this was a small asteroid and we're done.
3. [ ] Otherwise, we need to spawn 2 new asteroids like so:
   1. [ ] Call `log_event("asteroid_split")` (be sure to import `log_event` at the top of the file).
   2. [ ] Call [`random.uniform`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/random.html#random.uniform) to generate a random angle between `20` and `50` degrees (be sure to import the standard `random` library at the top of the file).
   3. [ ] Call the [`.rotate`](https://www.pygame.org/docs/ref/math.html#pygame.math.Vector2.rotate) method on the asteroid's `velocity` vector to create a _new_ vector representing the first new asteroids movement.
   4. [ ] Call the [`.rotate`](https://www.pygame.org/docs/ref/math.html#pygame.math.Vector2.rotate) again for the second new asteroid, but this time rotate it in the opposite direction (negative angle).
   5. [ ] Compute the new radius of the smaller asteroids using the formula `old_radius - ASTEROID_MIN_RADIUS`.
   6. [ ] Create two new `Asteroid` objects at the current asteroid position with the new radius.
   7. [ ] Set the first's `.velocity` to the first new vector, but make it move faster by scaling it up (multiplying) by `1.2`.
   8. [ ] Do the same for the second asteroid, but with the second new vector.
   9. [ ] In the game loop in `main.py`, replace `asteroid.kill()` with `asteroid.split()`.
   10. [ ] Run the game and shoot some asteroids!

If things seem to be working, **run and submit** the CLI tests.

title: Submit Your Repo
courseTitle: Build Asteroids using Python and Pygame
readme:
# Submit Your Repo

_You've done it_!

Once you're satisfied with your game, [push it up to GitHub](https://docs.github.com/en/get-started/using-git/pushing-commits-to-a-remote-repository), then submit the link to your project! Your link should look something like this: `https://github.com/github-username/repo-name`.

I hope you've enjoyed this project. It's always good to solidify the things you learn in courses by getting your hands dirty building something "real."

## Extending the Project

You've done all the required steps, but if you'd like to make the game your own, here are some ideas:

- Add a scoring system
- Implement multiple lives and respawning
- Add an explosion effect for the asteroids
- Add acceleration to the player movement
- Make the objects wrap around the screen instead of disappearing
- Add a background image
- Create different weapon types
- Make the asteroids lumpy instead of perfectly round
- Make the ship have a triangular hit box instead of a circular one
- Add a shield power-up
- Add a speed power-up
- Add bombs that can be dropped

Have fun with it, and make sure to share any improvements that you make with us in the [Discord](https://www.boot.dev/community)!
